first_paragraph: [Snowboarding](/wiki/Snowboarding "Snowboarding") is a [sport](/wiki/Olympic_sports "Olympic sports") at the [Winter Olympic Games](/wiki/Winter_Olympic_Games "Winter Olympic Games"). It was first included in the [1998 Winter Olympics](/wiki/1998_Winter_Olympics "1998 Winter Olympics") in Nagano, Japan.[[1]](#cite_note-1) Snowboarding was one of five new sports or disciplines added to the Winter Olympic program between 1992 and 2002, and was the only one not to have been a previous medal or demonstration event.[[2]](#cite_note-SBHist-2) In 1998, four events, two for men and two for women, were held in two specialities: the [Giant slalom](/wiki/Snowboard_racing#Giant_slalom "Snowboard racing"), a downhill event similar to [giant slalom skiing](/wiki/Giant_slalom_skiing "Giant slalom skiing"); and the [half-pipe](/wiki/Half-pipe "Half-pipe"), in which competitors perform tricks while going from one side of a semi-circular ditch to the other.[[2]](#cite_note-SBHist-2) [Canadian](/wiki/Canada_at_the_1998_Winter_Olympics "Canada at the 1998 Winter Olympics") [Ross Rebagliati](/wiki/Ross_Rebagliati "Ross Rebagliati") won the men's giant slalom and became the first athlete to win a gold medal in snowboarding.[[3]](#cite_note-3) Rebagliati was briefly stripped of his medal by the [International Olympic Committee](/wiki/International_Olympic_Committee "International Olympic Committee") (IOC) after testing positive for [marijuana](/wiki/Marijuana "Marijuana"). However, the IOC's decision was reverted following an appeal from the Canadian Olympic Association.[[4]](#cite_note-4) For the [2002 Winter Olympics](/wiki/2002_Winter_Olympics "2002 Winter Olympics"), giant slalom was expanded to add head-to-head racing and was renamed [Parallel giant slalom](/wiki/Snowboard_racing#Parallel_giant_slalom "Snowboard racing").[[5]](#cite_note-5) In [2006](/wiki/2006_Winter_Olympics "2006 Winter Olympics"), a third event, the snowboard cross, was held for the first time. [Snowboarding](/wiki/Snowboarding "Snowboarding") is a [sport](/wiki/Olympic_sports "Olympic sports") at the [Winter Olympic Games](/wiki/Winter_Olympic_Games "Winter Olympic Games"). It was first included in the [1998 Winter Olympics](/wiki/1998_Winter_Olympics "1998 Winter Olympics") in Nagano, Japan.[[1]](#cite_note-1) Snowboarding was one of five new sports or disciplines added to the Winter Olympic program between 1992 and 2002, and was the only one not to have been a previous medal or demonstration event.[[2]](#cite_note-SBHist-2) In 1998, four events, two for men and two for women, were held in two specialities: the [Giant slalom](/wiki/Snowboard_racing#Giant_slalom "Snowboard racing"), a downhill event similar to [giant slalom skiing](/wiki/Giant_slalom_skiing "Giant slalom skiing"); and the [half-pipe](/wiki/Half-pipe "Half-pipe"), in which competitors perform tricks while going from one side of a semi-circular ditch to the other.[[2]](#cite_note-SBHist-2) [Canadian](/wiki/Canada_at_the_1998_Winter_Olympics "Canada at the 1998 Winter Olympics") [Ross Rebagliati](/wiki/Ross_Rebagliati "Ross Rebagliati") won the men's giant slalom and became the first athlete to win a gold medal in snowboarding.[[3]](#cite_note-3) Rebagliati was briefly stripped of his medal by the [International Olympic Committee](/wiki/International_Olympic_Committee "International Olympic Committee") (IOC) after testing positive for [marijuana](/wiki/Marijuana "Marijuana"). However, the IOC's decision was reverted following an appeal from the Canadian Olympic Association.[[4]](#cite_note-4) For the [2002 Winter Olympics](/wiki/2002_Winter_Olympics "2002 Winter Olympics"), giant slalom was expanded to add head-to-head racing and was renamed [Parallel giant slalom](/wiki/Snowboard_racing#Parallel_giant_slalom "Snowboard racing").[[5]](#cite_note-5) In [2006](/wiki/2006_Winter_Olympics "2006 Winter Olympics"), a third event, the snowboard cross, was held for the first time. In this event, competitors race against each other down a course with jumps, beams and other obstacles.[[6]](#cite_note-6) On July 11, 2011, the [International Olympic Committee](/wiki/International_Olympic_Committee "International Olympic Committee")'s Executive Board approved the addition of Ski and Snowboard [Slopestyle](/wiki/Slopestyle "Slopestyle") to the [Winter Olympics](/wiki/Winter_Olympics "Winter Olympics") roster of events, effective in [2014](/wiki/2014_Winter_Olympics "2014 Winter Olympics"). The decision was announced via press conference from the IOC's meeting in Durban, South Africa. A fifth event, parallel slalom, was added only for 2014. Big air was added for 2018.

New Zealander [Zoi Sadowski-Synnott](/wiki/Zoi_Sadowski-Synnott "Zoi Sadowski-Synnott") is the most decorated snowboarder in Olympic history with 5 Olympic medals, followed by Austrian [Benjamin Karl](/wiki/Benjamin_Karl "Benjamin Karl") who has 4 Olympic medals. Ten athletes have won three medals: Americans [Shaun White](/wiki/Shaun_White "Shaun White"), [Jamie Anderson](/wiki/Jamie_Anderson_(snowboarder) "Jamie Anderson (snowboarder)"), [Lindsey Jacobellis](/wiki/Lindsey_Jacobellis "Lindsey Jacobellis") and [Kelly Clark](/wiki/Kelly_Clark "Kelly Clark"), as well as Canadians [Max Parrot](/wiki/Max_Parrot "Max Parrot") and [Mark McMorris](/wiki/Mark_McMorris "Mark McMorris"), Japanese [Ayumu Hirano](/wiki/Ayumu_Hirano "Ayumu Hirano"), Slovenian [Žan Košir](/wiki/%C5%BDan_Ko%C5%A1ir "Žan Košir"), Russian [Vic Wild](/wiki/Vic_Wild "Vic Wild") and Korean-American [Chloe Kim](/wiki/Chloe_Kim "Chloe Kim").
